claim: When a dog is flooded with adrenaline (fear/flight state), it makes lasting fear-associations with stimuli: the first person who calls, whistles, or pats their leg during a fearful encounter conditions the dog to bolt from those exact cues in future, even when the cue-maker is the owner.
stance_target: scientific basis for never-call/never-chase rule — adrenaline conditioning mechanism
scope_conditions: Initial encounter with a dog already in fight-or-flight state
quoted_support: 'When dogs are flooded with adrenaline, they are very reactive, and they make associations with things,' she explains. 'The first person who encounters a lost dog might call the dog or pat their leg or whistle, and if the dog is already in that fight or flight mode, he will likely panic.' The dog may then associate those overtures with something frightening and overwhelming, and bolt whenever he hears them, even if it's his owner who makes them; he may simply react without processing any of that information.

claim: Moving directly toward a scared dog in a straight line while standing upright and making eye contact mimics predator approach posture and increases flight probability.
stance_target: oblique, low, indirect approach as the correct technique; straight-line approach as counterproductive
scope_conditions: Approach to any scared/stressed dog in the field
quoted_support: 'You're not very likely to catch a dog if you are moving toward him in a direct line, standing ramrod straight and staring at him. What you're doing with this is mimicking a predator,' Albrecht says.

claim: Calming signals — yawning, blinking, peripheral-vision gaze (not direct stare), oblique approach (from the side, not head-on) — communicate peaceful intent to a dog and reduce flight response.
stance_target: calming-signal approach technique as effective for reducing dog fear response
scope_conditions: Approach to frightened/stressed dog; derived from intraspecific dog communication theory
quoted_support: Calming signals include yawning, using peripheral vision and blinking (never long, direct stares), and oblique approaches (moving from the side, not head on).
entities: On Talking Terms with Dogs: Calming Signals

claim: Grabbing or lunging at a loose scared dog commonly causes bites; opening a car door and allowing the dog to enter voluntarily is safer than physical restraint.
scope_conditions: Physical capture attempt of panicked dog
quoted_support: 'If you lunge, dogs may bite because they're afraid,' says Bonnie Folz of Howard Beach, New York. ...That lady would have been better off opening her car door and trying to entice the dog to jump in, using the vehicle as a 'trap' of sorts until help could be summoned.

claim: Albrecht's food-distraction lure technique — lying or sitting on the ground, pretending to eat from a crinkly bag filled with hot dogs, making lip-smacking sounds, watching only peripherally — can take 45 minutes to over an hour to draw in a scared dog.
stance_target: patience-based lure as effective technique for extremely shy/traumatized dogs
scope_conditions: Dog in evasion/avoidance mode; open field or park setting
quoted_support: Albrecht recommends sitting down, or even lying down flat with the food on your stomach, and watching the dog only from your peripheral vision — again, no staring. 'It could take 45 minutes to an hour, or longer, for the dog to advance toward you.'

claim: A dog that has been evasive for months can have an immediate recognition response (whining, tail-wagging) when it catches the owner's scent at close range, even after prolonged feral/evasion behavior.
stance_target: scent-recognition as a late-stage recovery mechanism after prolonged evasion
scope_conditions: Owner vs. stranger approach; close enough for scent detection
quoted_support: Some dogs will catch the scent of a familiar person and 'go through an immediate recognition,' Albrecht says. 'Owners describe case after case where a dog starts whining and wagging his tail' after recognizing 'his' human at long last.

claim: Chasing a dog on a highway causes it to run faster, often directly into oncoming traffic; the correct response is to follow with flashers on or pull over and call highway patrol.
stance_target: never-chase rule applies with extra force in traffic situations
quoted_support: The same applies if you see a dog wandering in traffic, in particular a highway or busy road: Don't jump out. Instead, put on your flashers and follow the dog as best you can, provided you do not create a traffic disturbance. Or pull over somewhere safe, and call highway patrol.

claim: Dogs that escape near shows or events (e.g. dog shows, airports) can remain loose and sighted for months without being caught when well-meaning rescuers use chase-and-call approaches, demonstrating the long-term failure mode of incorrect behavior.
stance_target: documented case of recovery failure from incorrect rescuer behavior
scope_conditions: High-stress disappearance (transport, show context); dog with sight-hound characteristics
quoted_support: She first got involved in finding lost dogs with the case of Vivi the Whippet, the dog who famously found herself loose on the tarmac at New York's John F. Kennedy Airport on the way home from competing at the Westminster dog show in 2006. Vivi was sighted for months, but never caught.
